Abrupt: sudden. (+ly) in a sudden manner, with no preparation
Acclaim: to praise or welcome sb/sth publicly
Acquit: to free/clear sb from a legal charge, declare innocent
Acronym: word formed from the first letter of a group of words
Act upon: proceed in accordance with what sth/ someone says
Adroit: skillful and clever, especially in dealing with people
Advocate: to defend by argument, to plead in favor of
Alibi: evidence that proves that sb was in another place at the time of a crime
Aloof: not friendly or interested in other people
Amateur: a person who takes part in an activity for enjoyment, not as a job
Amend: rectify something to make it greater, more intense, better
Ammunition: a supply of bullets, etc. To be fired from guns
Answer for: to be held responsible for something
Appall: to shock sb very much, horrify
As for: with regard to, with respect to, concerning
Ascend: to rise; to go up; to climb up
Ascribe: to credit or attribute to a cause or source
Aspiration: a strong desire to have or do sth
Assailant: someone who attacks someone else violently
Astonish: to amaze or surprise somebody to a great degree
Astray: off the right way into error, in the wrong direction
Audacious: willing to take risks or to do sth shocking, daring
Catchy: something easy to remember, that attracts interest
Charge for: to ask an amount as a price
Chaste: not having sex with anyone; only having sex with the person that you are married to
Check in: a place where you report your arrival, an airport, hotel
Cheer up: to become or make somebody happy
Choke: prevent so from breathing due to a blocked throat
Chuckle: to laugh softly in a low tone
Cinder: a small piece of ash or partly burnt coal, wood, etc. That is no longer burning but may still be hot
Cloak: to deliberately hide facts so people don’t see or understand
Come off: to be able to be removed; to be successful; to fall from sth
Come round to: change your opinion so you agree with someone
Competent: having enough skill or knowledge to do sth well or to the necessary standard
Compile: collect and reorganize material from different sources
Comply with: act in accordance with a command, rule, to obey
Conspicuous: easy to see or notice; likely to attract attention
Counterfeit: made to look exactly like something else
Crave for: to desire something greatly, to have cravings for
Cripple: to damage sb’s body so that they are no longer able to walk or move normally, disable
Crumble: to break or break sth into very small pieces
Beat up: to hit or kick sb hard, many times
Beguile: to trick sb into doing sth, especially by being nice to them; to attract or interest sb
Bequeath: to leave by will, to hand something down by will
Bewilder: to confuse or puzzle someone completely
Birch: a tree with smooth bark and thin branches
Bitter: very serious and unpleasant, with a lot of anger and hatred involved; having a strong, unpleasant taste
Bleach: to make something white or lighter in color
Blunt: without a sharp edge or point; saying exactly what you think without trying to be polite
Boo: to express dissatisfaction at a speaker or performer
Bounce: if sth bounces or you bounce it, it moves quickly away from a surface it has just hit or you make it do this
Bound: a place is out of bounds if you aren’t allowed to go into it
Braid: a long piece of sth, especially hair, that is divided into three parts and twisted, together
Breach: a failure to do sth that must be done by law; a break in a relationship between people or countries
Bring up: to raise from childhood; to mention a topic
Brink: an edge at the top of a steep place
Brush up on: to review something to refresh one’s memory
Call it a day: to stop working for the rest of the day
Candor: the quality of saying what you think openly and honestly, frankness
Carcass: the dead body of an animal
Carry out: to accomplish, to put something into practice
Catch up with: reach the same stage as somebody
Dodge: to move quickly and suddenly to one side in order to avoid sb/sth
Downgrade: lower the status or salary of someone
Drag: pull something heavy, to carry it along the ground
Drain: to make liquid flow away from something
Drastic: extreme in a way that has a sudden, serious or violent effect on sth
Drench: to make sb/sth completely wet, soak
Drought: period of dry weather when there is not rain
Drown: die or kill a person or animal by immersion in water
Dwell: to reside, to live as a resident
Dwindle: grow short, become gradually smaller, shorter
Eccentric: considered by other people to be strange or unusual
Ember: a piece of wood or coal that is not burning. but is still red and hot after a fire has died
Enchant: to attract sb strongly and make them feel very interested, excited, etc., delight
Endow: to give a large sum of money to an institution to provide it with an income
Enhance: to increase or further improve the good quality, value or status of sb/sth
Enlist: to persuade sb to help you or to join you in doing sth
Entreat: to ask sb to do sth in a serious and often emotional way, beg, implore
Escort: a person or group of people that travels with sb/sth to protect or guard them
Eventually: at the end of a period of time or a series of events
Evict: expel/put out a tenant by legal process
Excel: to be very good at doing sth, to do even better than you usually do
Excerpt: a short piece taken from a book, piece of music
Excuse: a reason, either true or invented that you give to defend your behavior
Decoy: person/thing used to trick somebody into danger
Defeat: to win against sb in a war, competition, sports game, etc., beat
Defy: to refuse to obey or show respect for sb in authority, a law, a rule, etc.
Delusion: a false belief or opinion about yourself or your situation, illusion
Demeanor: the way that sb looks or behaves
Dense: containing a lot of people, things, plants, etc. With little space between them
Density: number of things/people in the area of a place
Deprive: to prevent sb from having or doing sth, especially sth important
Detach: to remove sth from sth larger; to become separated from sth
Detain: to hold in or as in custody
Devote: to give most of your time, energy, attention, etc. To sb/sth
Dilute: to make a liquid weaker by adding water or another liquid to it
Disabled: sb who cannot use a part of their body properly
Discard: to throw away, to get rid of something unwanted
Disclose: to give sb information about sth, especially sth that was previously secret, reveal
Disfigure: to spoil the appearance of something
Dismiss: to direct or allow to leave, to discharge from service
Dispatch: to send sb/sth somewhere, especially for a special purpose
Disperse: to move apart and go away in diff. erent directions; to make sb/sth do this
Displeye: to make sb feel upset, annoyed or not satisfied
Disposal/dispose of: get rid of something hard to get rid of
Divert: to make sb/sth change direction, to take sb’s thoughts or attention away from sth distract
Exploit: to treat a person as an opportunity to gain an advantage for yourself
Fable: a traditional short story that teaches a moral lesson
Falsehood: the state of not being true; the act of telling a lie
Falter: to become weaker or less effective, waver
Famine: a lack of food during a long period of time in a region
Feat: remarkable act involving courage, and skills
Feature: something important, interesting or typical of a place or thing
Fee: an amount of money that you pay for professional advice or services
Feel like: to have the desire to do/eat something
Flatter: pleased because someone shows you they admire you
Flee: to leave very quickly because of danger
Flicker: to burn or shine in a weak unsteady way
Flip: to turn something over from one side to the other
Flirt: to behave towards sb as if you find them sexually attractive
Fold: to bend sth, especially paper or cloth
Footstep: the sound or mark made each time your foot touches the ground
Forage: (especially of an animal) to search for food, rummage
Foreman: a worker who is in charge of a group of other building workers
Forge: to make an illegal copy of sth in order to cheat people
Former: happening or existing before, but not now
Freckle: harmless small brownish patch on somebody’s skin
Fringe: the front part of sb’s hair that is cut so that it hangs over their forehead
Frolic: to play in a lively, happy way
Futile: having no purpose because there is no chance of success, pointless
Gauze: light transparent cloth, made of cotton
Gaze at: look steadily at somebody/something in amazement
Get across: to be communicated or understood; to succeed in communicating sth
Get along: to have a friendly relationship with
Get away with: do something wrong without being punished
Get cold feet: to suddenly become nervous about doing sth
Get in: to enter or board a car
Get off: to descend from a train, a plane, a bus
Get off (send): to send, to mail, to escape usual punishment
Get on with: to resume what you were doing, continue
Get on: to board a bus, a train, to grow old
Get out of: free oneself from obligation, avoid doing it
Get through: to make one’s way in a crowd
Gill: one of the openings on the side of a fish’s head that it breathes through
Gist: the main or general meaning of a piece of writing or a conversation
Give away: reveal a secret. To give a present
Give by: to employ, to make use of
Give off: to produce sth such as a smell, heat, light, etc.
Give up: to renounce, to abandon, to fail to finish
Glimpse: sight of so/sth for a short and incomplete time
Gloomy: nearly dark, or badly lit in a way that makes you feel sad, depressing
Go back on: to fail to keep a promise; to change your mind about sth
Go in for: to engage in, to participate in
Go off: to explode, to leave, to terminate
Go over: to repeat, to examine, to be successful
Go through: to experience or suffer sth, to look at or examine sth carefully
Goblet: a cup for wine, made of glass or metal
Gossip: rumor, report of an intimate nature
Gourmet: producing or connected with good food and drink
Grab: to take or hold sb/sth with your hand suddenly, firmly or roughly, seize
Grant somebody sth: give so/sth what they have asked for
Grope: to try and find sth that you cannot see, by feeling with your hands
Grudge: give or allow reluctantly or resentfully
Halt: to stop; to make sb/sth stop
Hand in: to submit, to deliver something due
Hang onto: to cling to, to remain attached
Hang up: to replace a telephone receiver on its hook
Haughty: unfriendly because you think that you are better than them, arrogant
Have a go: to make an attempt to do sth
Hazy: not clear because of haze; not clear because of a lack of memory
Heal: wound/broken bone growing back together healthy
Hijack: use violence to take control of a plane, etc.
Hinge: a piece of metal, plastic, etc. On which a door, lid or gate
Hoard: a supply or fund stored up and often hidden away
Hoarse: sounding rough and unpleasant, especially because of a sore throat
Hoax: an act intended to make sb believe sth that is not true
Hoist: to raise or pull sth up to a higher position, often using ropes
Hold out for: continue a dispute or struggle for
Hold up: to delay, to last, to rob, to survive
Host: provide place/what is needed for an organized event
Hover: to remain floating, fluttering suspended in the air
Hurl: to throw sth/sb violently in a particular direction
Husk: the dry outer covering of nuts, fruits and seeds
Idle: (of people) not working hard, lazy, (of machines) not in use
In the long run: concerning a longer period in the future, eventually
Inconceivable: impossible to imagine or believe, unthinkable
Indelible: impossible to forget or remove, permanent
Inedible: that you cannot eat because it is of poor quality, or poisonous
Ineligible: disqualified by law to be elected
Influx: the fact of a lot of people, money or things arriving somewhere
Interchange: the act of sharing or exchanging sth
Jeopardy: in a dangerous position or situation and likely to be lost or harmed
Juggle: to try to deal with two or more, things or activities at the same time
Keep up with: to move, make progress or increase at the same rate as sb/sth
Knock sth off: stop doing sth; cut it off
Lag: to move or develop more slowly than other people, organizations, etc.
Latch: a small metal bar that is used to fasten a door or a gate
Lay: (laid, laid) to put sb/sth in a particular position
Let off the hook: to free yourself or sb else from a difficult situation
Lie (horizontal): (lay, lain) to be or put yourself in a flat or horizontal position
Lie (untruth): (lied, lied) to say or write sth that you know is not true
Limb: an arm or leg, a large branch of a tree
Limp: to walk slowly or with difficulty because one leg is injured
Live up to: achieve what is expected of oneself
Lofty: (of buildings, mountains, etc.) Very high and impressive
Log: to make an official record of events; facts
Long: to want sth that does not seem likely to happen soon, yearn
Look back on: to think about sth in your past
Look down on: to think that you are better than sb/sth
Lump: a swelling under the skin, sometimes a sign of serious illness
Lure: to persuade or trick sb to do sth by promising them a reward, entice
Make up: to form sth, constitute; to invent a story; to compensate for sth
Mend: to repair a tear or hole in a piece of clothing
Mild: not severe or strong; not very cold, and therefore pleasant
Mischief: behavior intended to cause trouble, prank, trick
Mist: a cloud of very small drops in the air above the ground, fog
Mound: a large pile of earth or stones; a small hill
Mouth‐watering: food that looks or smells extremely good
Muffin: a type of round flat bread roll, usually eaten hot with butter
Nag: insult, complain to somebody in an irritating way
Nameless: having no name, not known by name
Nap: a short sleep, especially during the day
Neglect: to not look after someone or sth
Nursery: a room in a house where young children can play
Offspring: a child of a particular person or couple; the young of an animal
On show: to be shown to the public
Outfit: the gear/clothes or equipment for a special purpose
Outlet: a shop that sells goods made by a particular company
Outrage: sth that strongly offends feelings; malicious
Outskirts: the parts of a town or city that are furthest from the center
Outspoken: expressing opinions even if not popular to do so
Overdue: not paid, done, returned, etc. By the required or expected time
Overexposed: exposed for too long, too exposed
Overwhelm: flow over the top/use superior strength of force
Pace: the speed at which you walk or run
Partition: a wall or screen that separates one part of a room from another
Pavilion: a temporary building used at public events and exhibitions
Paw: the foot of an animal that had claws
Pebble: a smooth, round stone that is found in or near water
Peril: great danger of being harmed or killed
Perish: to die, especially in a sudden violent way
Peruse: to examine by reading, to scrutinize
Pervade: to spread through and be noticeable in every part of sth, permeate
Pew: a long wooden seat in a church
Pierce: to make a hole, to perforate
Pinch: to hold sth tightly between the thumb and finger
Plead: to ask for sth you want very much emotionality
Pledge: firm promise/agreement made publicly or officially
Plough: a large piece of farming equipment with one or several curved blades
Plump: having a soft, round body; slightly fat
Pose: raise a question, as for a portrait/picture
Praise: to express your approval or admiration for sb/sth, compliment
Precedent: an official action or decision that has happened in the past, example
Precipitate: to make sth, especially sth bad, happen suddenly or sooner
Prick: to make a very small hole in sth with a sharp point
Profess: to state openly that you have a particular belief, feeling, etc., declare
Promote: to help sth to happen or develop; to move sb to a higher rank
Prompt: being ready and quick to act, punctual
Propagate: to spread an idea, a belief or a piece of information among many people
Prostrate: stretched out with face on the ground, flat
Provincial: connected with the parts of a country that do not include the capital city
Provoke: to cause a particular reaction or have a particular effect
Pursue: keep doing /trying to achieve sth over and over
Put away: to put sth in the place where it is usually kept
Put down: criticize so, front/pay part of sth/to write
Put off: to change sth to a later time or date, postpone, delay
Put through: connect someone to someone else on the phone
Put up with: to accept sb/sth that is annoying without complaining, tolerate
Quaint: attractive in an unusual or old‐fashioned way
Query: a question, especially one asking for information or expressing a doubt about sth
Rare: not found or seen very often, scarce; not cooked
Rate: the speed at which sth happens over a period of time
Ratio: the relationship between two groups that is represented by two numbers
Rattle: to make a series of clattering, knocking sounds
Reckless: worthless, lack of thought of danger
Record: to keep a permanent account of facts by writing them down, filming them, etc.
Refrain: to stop yourself from doing sth, especially sth that you want to do, desist from
Reliable: that can be trusted to do sth well; that you can rely on, dependable
Relic: an object, a tradition, a system, etc. That has survived from the past
Repeal: if a government or person with authority repeals a law, that law is no longer valid
Reproach: to blame or criticize sb for sth that they have done or not done
Reprove: to tell sb that you do not approve of sth that they have done, rebuke
Repulse: to make sb feel disgust or dislike, repel
Resent: to feel bitter or angry about sth, especially because you feel it is unfair
Revert to: to go back to a state
Revive: to become, or to make sb/sth become, conscious or healthy and strong again
Rigid: very strict and difficult to change
Rivulet: a very small river; a small stream of water or other liquid
Roam: to walk or travel around an area without any definite aim or direction, wander
Rotten: affected by rot or decay/unpleasant, unfortunate
Rubble: a mass of stone or bricks from a destroyed building
Ruffles: strips of pleated fabric used for decoration
Run across: to meet sb or find sth by chance
Sage: wise, especially because you have a lot of experience
Saucer: a small shallow round dish that a cup stands on; an object that is shaped like this
Savor: to enjoy the full taste or flavor of sth, especially by eating it slowly, relish
Scald: to burn yourself or part of your body with very hot liquid or steam
Scan: to read fast to find some information, fast movement
Scant: hardly any; not very much and not as much as there should be
Scanty: too little in amount for what is needed
Scoff at: to talk about sb/sth in a way that makes it clear that you think they are stupid or ridiculous, mock, laugh at
Scruple: a feeling that prevents you from doing sth that you think may be morally wrong
Secluded: private/quiet place a long way from others, private
Sentry: a soldier whose job is to guard sth
Set out: to leave a place and begin a journey; to present ideas in an organized way
Sham: a situation, feeling, system, etc. That is not as good or true as it seems to be
Shanty: a small house, built of pieces of wood, metal and cardboard, where very poor people live
Shape up: to improve your behavior, work harder, etc.
Shatter: to break suddenly into small pieces
Shimmer: to shine softly with a wavering, flickering light
Shiver: tremor, shudder caused by fear, cold or illness
Show up: to arrive where you have arranged to meet sb or do sth; appear
Shrewd: clever at understanding and making judgements about a situation
Shrug: to raise one’s shoulders in indifference
Sign: an event, a fact, etc. that shows that sth exists, is happening or may happen, indication
Silence: a complete lack of noise or sound
Slide: to move smoothly over a surface while touching it
Slip: a small mistake, (v): to lose stability
Slipper: a loose soft shoe that you wear in the house
Slippery: difficult to hold or to stand or move on, because it is smooth, wet or polished
Soar: ascend suddenly above the usual level, fly/glide high
Spill: liquids that flow over the edge of a container
Spurn: to reject with disdain or contempt
Stack: to pile up large quantity of something
Startle: to surprise provoking so to jump in fright
Step down: to leave a top position, to resign
Stoical: unaffected by pleasure or pain, impassive
Stork: a large black and white bird with a long beak and neck and long legs
Story: an account of what happened to sb; a level of a building; a floor
Strait: a narrow passage of water that connects two seas or large areas of water
Strap: to wrap strips of material around an injured part of the body, bandage
Stray: to deviate from a straight, proper course
Stride across: to walk quickly with long steps
Strife: angry or violent disagreement between two people or groups of people
Strike: you realize something is important, surprising, bad
Strive: to try very hard to achieve sth
Stumble: to walk unsteadily, as if intoxicated
Stun: surprise/upset someone so they don’t react immediately
Stunt: a dangerous action to entertain people, especially as part of a film/movie
Subdue: to bring sb/sth under control, especially by using force, defeat
Submit: to obey so. when you have no choice
Subordinate: less important than sth else, secondary
Subsidize: pay part of the cost of sth so the buyer pays less
Subsist: to manage to stay alive, especially with limited food or money
Summon: order someone to come to a meeting, a court of law
Suspend: to officially stop sth for a time
Swallow: make food/drink go down your throat
Sway: move from one side to the other, to influence someone
Swell: become larger than normal because of illness or injury
Swerve: to change direction suddenly, especially in order to avoid hitting sb/sth
Sword: a hand held weapon with a long, sharp blade
Take after: resemble so in appearance/character, to look like
Take down: to write down, to put down
Take hold off: to grasp, to grip with hands
Take over: assume the control or management of something
Take something for granted: consider sth as true, as real
Take up: deal with/to enter a profession, business, sport
Tangle: to twist sth into an untidy mass
Tear: a hole in a piece of cloth, paper
Tease: to torment, to disturb, to irritate
Testify: to make a statement that sth happened or that sth is true, especially as a witness in court
Thaw: to turn back into water after being frozen, melt
Thrifty: careful about spending money and not wasting things, frugal
Toll: money that you pay to use a particular road or bridge
Transition: the process or a period of changing from one state or condition to another
Transmit: to send an electronic signal, radio or television broadcast, etc.
Treacherous: that cannot be trusted; intending to harm you, deceitful
Unbearable: too painful, annoying or unpleasant to deal with or accept
Undertake: to take upon oneself as a task, duty
Upgrade: move up to a higher grade, promote
Uphold: to support sth that you think is right and make sure that it continues to exist
Uset: to make sb/yourself feel unhappy, anxious or annoyed, distress
Vanish: to disappear suddenly and/or in a way that you cannot explain
Veto: the right to refuse to allow sth to be done, especially the right to stop a law from being passed
Vie: to compete eagerly, passionately, with sb for sth; to contend
Wade: to walk through shallow waters, a river
Wag: if a dog wags its tail, or its tail wags, its tail moves from side to side several times
Wander: to walk slowly, often without any particular sense of purpose or direction
Warble: (of a bird) to sing with rapidly changing notes
Ward off: avert, prevent something from harming someone
Warn sb about sth: caution, prevent sb/sth about sth bad/dangerous; advice about danger
Warp: to become, or make sth become, twisted or bent out of its natural shape
Weave: to make cloth by passing threads under and over
Whimper: to make low, weak crying noises; to speak in this way
Withdraw: to move back or away from a place or situation; to make sb/sth do this, pull out
Wither: if a plant withers or sth withers it, it dries up and dies
Witty: able to say or write clever, amusing things
Work out: to exercise, to develop/devise a plan
Wrinkle: a line or small fold in your skin, especially on your face, that forms as you get older
